coredata.dc:title: Bamboo prepared carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for enhancing Bi3Ti4O12 nanosheets photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Tao
Liu, Xiqing
Ma, Changchang
Zhu, Zhi
Liu, Yang
Liu, Zhi
Wei, Maobin
Zhao, Xiaoxun
Dong, Hongjun
Huo, Pengwei
Li, Chunxiang
Yan, Yongsheng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel, simple, economical and green carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were obtained by hydrothermal method using bamboo as a carbon source at the first time. In order to further the use of such CQDs, we reported the facile fabrication of CQDs/Bi3Ti4O12 (BTO) nanosheets photocatalysts. The experimental results indicated that CQDs were successfully coupled with BTO nanosheets, exhibiting excellent photocatalytic activity than bare BTO. The improved photodegradation efficiency was attributed to the extended light absorption range, and increased separation rate of electron-holes pairs. At last, a possible photocatalytic mechanism was proposed. The design of such CQDs from the creature with semiconductor can be extended to photocatalytic systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bamboo
Hydrothermal
Carbon quantum dots
Bi3Ti4O12
Nanosheets

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs modified Bi4Ti3O12 nanosheets photocatalyst
Optical and electronics of CQDs/BTO photocatalysts

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns (a) and FT-IR patterns (b) of BTO and 3 wt % CQDs/BTO.
(a) SEM images of CQDs/BTO. (b, c) TEM images of CQDs/BTO. (d) HRTEM images of CQDs/BTO and the EDS mapping of Bi (e), Ti (f), O (g) and C (h).
High-resolution XPS spectra of Bi (a), Ti (b), O (c) and C (d) spectrum for CQDs/BTO samples.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs/BTO and (b) (αEphoton)1/2 vs Ephoton curves of CQDs/BTO.
Photocurrent response (a) EIS spectra (b) of BTO and CQDs/BTO photocatalysts.
PL spectra curves of BTO and 3 wt % CQDs/BTO samples.

N2 adsorption–desorption curves of BTO (red) and 3 wt % CQDs/BTO (black) samples. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Photocatalytic activity of CQDs/BTO based on the photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin. (b) Kinetic fit for the degradation with the bare BTO and CQDs/BTO samples. (c) The repeated photocatalytic experiments for 3 wt % CQDs/BTO. (d) The XRD spectrum of 3 wt % CQDs/BTO before and after 4 cycles photocatalytic experiments.
Valence-band XPS spectra of the 3 wt % CQDs/BTO materials.
DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra of 3 wt % CQDs/BTO samples in (a) methanol and (b) water under visible light irradiation.
Comparison of photocatalytic activities of 3 wt % CQDs/BTO materials for degradation with or without adding EDTA-2Na, N2 and IPA.
Schematic illustration of the charge separation and transfer behavior of charge carriers and photocatalytic reaction mechanism over 3 wt % CQDs/BTO.

head.ce:title: Bamboo prepared carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for enhancing Bi3Ti4O12 nanosheets photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, 212013 Zhenjiang, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University, 212013 Zhenjiang, PR China

School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics, Jilin Normal University, 13600 Siping, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment and Safety Engineering, Liaoning Normal University, 116029, Dalian, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, 212013 Zhenjiang, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu Province, 86-212000, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel, simple, economical and green carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were obtained by hydrothermal method using bamboo as a carbon source at the first time. In order to further the use of such CQDs, we reported the facile fabrication of CQDs/Bi3Ti4O12 (BTO) nanosheets photocatalysts. The experimental results indicated that CQDs were successfully coupled with BTO nanosheets, exhibiting excellent photocatalytic activity than bare BTO. The improved photodegradation efficiency was attributed to the extended light absorption range, and increased separation rate of electron-holes pairs. At last, a possible photocatalytic mechanism was proposed. The design of such CQDs from the creature with semiconductor can be extended to photocatalytic systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon materials have been appealed much attention due to their wide applications in adsorption, electrochemistry, separation [1,2]. Recently, carbon materials have been activity considered as the most promising candidates in photocatalytic technology. Particularly, considerable research efforts have been focused on the preparation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and their application in photocatalysis [3,4]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), can be amorphous or nanocrystalline with sp2 carbon clusters and may fuse with some diamond-like structure formed by sp3 carbons [5,6]. They have greatly attracted interests of researchers in recent years due to their superiority, and have been introduced to several semiconductors such as TiO2, BiOCl, BiOI and BiVO4, and have been demonstrated to be an effective approach to improve photocatalytic activity [4,7–10]. Nonetheless, these traditional approaches for CQDs suffer from disadvantages, such as involvement of complex process or the used of expensive raw materials. Recently, carbonization of chitosan, glucose, grass and coffee grounds have been employed to prepare carbon nanoparticle [11,12]. From the point of view of material synthesis, it is highly desirable to explore a new carbon source for green, abundant, economical and applied in photocatalysis.

Recently, bismuth titanate (Bi4Ti3O12, BTO) nanosheet has been demonstrated to be promising photocatalyst for the removal of organic pollutants. It consists with pseudo-perovskite (Bi2Ti3O10)2- units sandwiched between the bismuth oxygen (Bi2O2)2+ layers along c axis. This unique structure feature can ensure the (Bi2O2)2+ and (Bi2Ti3O10)2- layers to forme an inner electric field to separate photo-generated electrons and holes, finally exhibiting excellent photocatalytic activity [13–16]. However, the efficiency of bare BTO was far from satisfaction due to the relative larger band gap (Eg = 2.95 eV) and high recombination rate of photo-generated electron-holes pairs. In order to improve its photocatalytic activity, many BTO-based hybrid photocatalyst (Bi4Ti3O12/BiOI, Bi4Ti3O12/SrTiO3, BiOCl/Bi4Ti3O12, Bi4Ti3O12/Bi2WO6) have been prepared and applied for the photodegradation [17–20]. However, it is still far from enough for potential applications and it was significant to find further improve the photocatalytic activity. Therefore, CQDs could be candidates to increase the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs. However, there have been few reports to study the coupling mode of CQDs and Bi4Ti3O12 nanosheets. The functional mechanism of CQDs for the improvement of activity remains ambiguous. And the photocatalystic mechanism of the CQDs-based photocatalysts for pollutant photodegradation remain needs to be further explored. Considering the excellent property of CQDs, the photocatalytic activity of Bi4Ti3O12 will improved if the CQDs were introduced to the Bi4Ti3O12 nanosheets.

In this article, we took the bamboo as a carbon source successfully synthesized carbon quantum dots and prepared novel CQDs modified Bi4Ti3O12 nanosheets photocatalyst. Remarkably, Bi4Ti3O12 photocatalyst containing CQDs feature notably enhanced photocatalytic activity. The key roles of CQDs in the complex photocatalyst exhibited the highly photocatalytic activity compared to bare Bi4Ti3O12. The CQDs was loaded on the surface of Bi4Ti3O12 can effectively improve the separation of electron-hole pairs and the up converted photoluminescence (PL) behavior of CQDs make the complex systems effectively utilize the sunlight to greatly enhance the photocatalytic activity.

NaCl, P25, C2H5OH, KCl and other chemicals were purchased from Aladin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used as received without further purification. The bamboo was derived from Jiangsu University (Zhenjiang, China) and washed with water for further use. The water used throughout all the experiments was purified through a Millipore system.

CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of bamboo. In a typical synthesis, bamboo (3 g) was added into H2O (60 mL). Then the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and hated at 200 °C for a period of 24 h. The CQDs were collected by removing the large dots through centrifugation and dialyzing. The CQDs thus obtained were dispersed in water for further characterization and use.

Briefly, Bi2O3, P25, NaCl and KCl with appropriate stoichiometric amounts were mixed in logical order and grinded well in crucible, after that, the mixture was calcined at 750 °C for 2.0 h in air [14]. After calcination, the final products were cooled to room temperature and milled into powders.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs modified Bi4Ti3O12 nanosheets photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical synthesis of CQDs modified Bi4Ti3O12 materials, a certain amount of CQDs were dissolved in 5 mL ethanol and ultrasonic dispersion. Then 0.1 g Bi4Ti3O12 was added into above solution and magnetic stirring at room temperature. After being stirred for 12 h, the suspension was heated at 60 °C, until the evaporation of water. The dried composite was heated at 200 °C for 1 h in a N2 atmosphere. The added contents of CQDs in the CQDs/Bi4Ti3O12 materials were 1, 3, 5 and 7 wt %, respectively.

X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) of the samples were recorded at room temperature, and the patterns of the photocatalyst were obtained with a D/max-RA X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) which equipped with Ni-filtrated Cu Ka radiation (40 kV, 200 mA) at 10–80° with a scanning step of 7°/min. The infrared spectra were obtained on a Nicolet Magna-IR 550 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer and KBr as the reference sample within wavelength range of 400–4000 cm−1. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were examined with JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded by a field emission scanning electron microscopy (Nova Nano SEM 450, FEI). The room-temperature photoluminescence (PL) spectra were investigated utilizing the (Cary Eclipse Spectrophotometer, VARIAN, USA) equipped with xenon (Xe) lamp with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were obtained using a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrometer by using BaSO4 as a reference. The UV–vis adsorption spectra of the samples were measured on a Cary 5000 (Agilent, USA) UV-VIS spectrophotometer. The electrochemical measurements were carried out on a CHI 760D (CH Instruments, Inc.) electrochemical workstation in a three-electrode system with a platinum network counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) reference electrode, using a concentration of 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous electrolyte under ambient temperature (25 °C). The transient photocurrent response was performed on the electrochemical analyzer using 0.5 V bias voltage under light irradiation (300 W Xe lamp as the light source).

50 mg photocatalyst was suspended in 100 mL aqueous solution of 15 mg/L ciprofloxacin. The solution was stirred for 30 min without light to ensure the establishment of an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. 5 mL solution was taken out in the container was irradiated by a 300 W Xe lamp as visible light source. At certain time intervals and analyzed on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer to record concentrations. The photocatalytic degradation rate was calculated using the following formula:
η% = (C0-Ct)/C0*100%
where C0 denotes the initial concentration balance value and Ct is the concentration value of t time.

In order to explore the effect of CQDs in improving the photocatalytic activity of BTO complex system, multiple techniques were employed. Fig. 1 a presented the XRD pattern of as-prepared BTO and CQDs/BTO photocatalysts exhibited the same characteristic diffraction peaks of perovskite phase Bi4Ti3O12 (PDF#305-0795). The position of the diffraction peaks of continues unchanged for CQDs/BTO, which means the introduction of the CQDs do not change the phase structure of BTO photocatalysts [21,22]. Moreover, no signal about CQDs can be detected, which can be attributed to the small amounts, high dispersion and low crystallinity of CQDs in the CQDs/BTO photocatalysts. The result can also be confirmed in similar system. From the FT-IR spectra of BTO and CQDs/BTO (Fig. 1b), we can see that the bands at 816.49 cm−1 and 576.07 cm−1 in BTO are attributed to the stretching vibrations of Bi-O and Ti-O. After CQDs modification, the intensity and peak width of these two peaks were changed, demonstrating that the CQDs was successfully coupled with BTO material. Such coupling will increase the separation of electron-hole pairs and improve the photocatalytic activity.

The morphology of CQDs/BTO samples were observed by SEM and TEM analysis. From the SEM image (Fig. 2 a), we can obviously find that the as-prepared CQDs/BTO was a 2D nanosheets feature. At the same time, we can also see numerous nano-fragments, which caused by ultrasonic during the preparation process. TEM analysis was applied to further reveal the morphology and microstructure of the CQDs/BTO composites (Fig. 2b–c). The near transparency of the nanosheets indicated the ultrathin structure and numerous nanodots can be seen, which implied the BTO was the ultrathin nanosheets and the CQDs have been coupled with the BTO successfully. The HRTEM image of CQDs/BTO photocatalysts were shown in Fig. 2d, and the lattice spacing of BTO was determined to be 0.410 nm, which were matched to the (080) crystal plane [23,24]. Meanwhile, some nanodots were steadily attached to the surface of BTO nanosheets, which were suggested the CQDs have been amended on the BTO nanosheets to form well amalgamation. Moreover, the EDS element mapping vividly displayed the elements Bi, Ti, O and C were regularly distributed in CQDs modified BTO photocatalysts (Fig. 2e–h). The results were a strong proof that we have successfully synthesized CQDs/BTO composites photocatalysts.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to investigate the chemical states of Bi, Ti, O and C. As Fig. 3 a–d shown, the high-resolution XPS spectrum that composites were composed of Bi, Ti, O and C for 3 wt % CQDs/BTO sample, respectively. Fig. 3a showed the binding energies of Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 peaks in the BTO materials were located at 158.8 eV and 164.0 eV, respectively, implying the Bi3+ in the crystal structure [25,26]. In Fig. 3b, XPS signals of Ti 2p were observed at binding energies around 465.0 eV (Ti 2p1/2) and 457.4 eV (Ti 2p3/2), which were well coincided with pure TiO2 [27,28]. The O 1s peak at 529.8 eV was attributed to the oxygen in BTO in BTO system. Fig. 3c showed the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, which suggested the surface functional groups on the CQDs. The main peak at 284.6 eV was attributed to the C-C bond with sp2 orbital and peaks centered at the binding energies of 285.9 eV and 287.4 eV were assigned to the oxygenated carbon and nitrous carbon (Fig. 3d), respectively. The result of XPS analysis denoted the concomitance of BTO and CQDs in the CQDs/BTO photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and electronics of CQDs/BTO photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a showed absorption spectra of the CQDs/BTO composite and the bare BTO. For the pure BTO, the sample has only little photoabsorption at visible light region, and the absorption edge was 403 nm. After the surface of BTO was modified with CQDs, the obtained CQDs/BTO photocatalysts exhibited wide visible-light absorption region and enhanced light absorption intensity. The results indicated that the CQDs coupled with BTO photocatalyst can be excited to produced more electron-hole pairs under the same light irradiation and thus improve the photocatalytic activity. The band gaps of the pure BTO and CQDs/BTO samples was calculated by means of Kubelka-Munk theory [22,29]. As shown in Fig. 4b, the band gap of the pure BTO photocatalyst was about 2.93 eV, while the introduction of CQDs lessened the band gaps, changing from 2.60 eV to 2.15 eV gradually. The results suggested that the CQDs have successfully coupled with BTO photocatalysts, which was correspond to the FT-IR. The reduced band gaps can excellent promote the electronic transition and improve the photocatalytic degradation process.

The prompt [30,31] response of the as-prepared materials corresponded well with on/off cycles of visible light irradiation (Fig. 5 a), which was a fundamental tool for characterizing the interfacial charge transfer properties of electrodes. A significantly higher photocurrent was seen for CQDs/BTO electron in comparison to pure BTO electron upon irradiation, which indicated that the composites materials can separate electron-hole pairs more effectively. It means that a faster interfacial charge transfer to the electron acceptor occurs and leading to effective separation of electron-hole pairs by the introduction of CQDs. For better understanding, (EIS) was also performed. Commonly, the charge transfer resistance of the electron surface was equal to the diameter of the semicircle and a smaller diameter means a lower resistance in the Nyquist plot [32,33]. From Fig. 5b, we can find that CQDs/BTO has a low resistance value as compared to BTO, that was consistence with the results of photocurrent and PL analysis.

Photoluminescence (PL) techniques were employed to investigate the transfer and recombination process of electrons and holes in the system [29,34]. The steady-state PL spectra of the bare BTO and 3 wt % CQDs/BTO samples were showed in Fig. 6 . The PL intensity with higher value means higher recombination possibility of electron-hole pairs. For CQDs/BTO composites, the emission intensity was much lower than that of pure BTO at similar emission position, which implying the modification of CQDs could effective reduced the recombination of electron-hole pairs.

As we all known, the surface areas also have great effect on the photocatalytic activity. Therefore, the specific surface areas of BTO and 3 wt % CQDs/BTO samples were measured by N2 adsorption-desorption analysis. Fig. 7 showed the corresponding N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms for BTO and 3 wt % CQDs/BTO photocatalysts, where the BET values were calculated of 14.617 and 11.321 m2/g for BTO and 3 wt % CQDs/BTO, respectively. Both of the samples exhibited typical type III isothermals with distinct H3-type hysteresis loop at P/P0 (IUPAC classification) [35,36]. The modification of CQDs leading to the reduction of BET surface area, which suggesting that the specific surface area was not the main factor to responsible for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of CQDs/BTO photocatalysts.

To evaluate the photocatalytic activities of the prepared BTO and the relaxed complex photocatalysts were explored for the degradation of ciprofloxacin at room temperature. Fig. 8 a displayed the degradation rates of ciprofloxacin over different samples. Among them, 3 wt % CQDs/BTO exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic degradation kinetics of ciprofloxacin was investigated (Fig. 8b), and was found the changes of the ciprofloxacin concentration versus the reaction time over the CQDs/BTO photocatalysts followed pseudo-first-order kinetics plot by the equation of –ln(C/C0) = kt [37,38]. The k value was the pseudo-first-order rate constant, C0 and C were the initial and the t time ciprofloxacin concentrations, respectively. It can be find that the 3 wt % CQDs/BTO composites displayed the higher rate constant than bare BTO, which is 0.01164 min−1. Furthermore, considering the reusability and stability of the photocatalysts are momentous for practical application, the cycle experiments of degradation were carried out and the materials after the degradation were checked out by XRD. The photocatalytic degradation ability of the CQDs/BTO composites have no apparent loss after four cycles (Fig. 8c). Meanwhile, the XRD of the CQDs/BTO before and after photodegradation has no clearly variation (Fig. 8d). All the above results demonstrated that the CQDs/BTO can serve as a kind of excellent photocatalytic material.

The photocatalytic mechanism of the as-prepared CQDs/BTO composites for the degradation of pollutants was further explored by analyzing the following experimental data. The valence band (VB) potentials of 3 wt % CQDs/BTO were determined by using XPS valence spectra and Fig. 9 showed the maximum energy edge of the VB were about 1.62 eV. Based on the DRS analysis (Fig. 4b), we can know the Eg was 2.50 eV. Moreover, the conduction band (CB) potential can be obtained by the formula ECB = EVB - Eg. Thus, the EVB of 3 wt % CQDs/BTO was calculated to be −0.88 eV. As we all known, the VB value was less positive than E0 (OH/OH−) (2.38 eV vs NHE), while the CB value less positive than E0 (O2/O2
-) (−0.046 eV vs NHE), which means the photogenerated holes of 3 wt % CQDs/BTO cannot oxidize OH− to yield OH and the photogenerated electrons cannot reduced O2 to generate O2
-. Based on the above analysis, it can be inferred that the O2
- may the main active species, yet the OH− was not the main active species and the direct hole may also dominate the photodegradation process.

The electron spin resonance (ESR) spin-trap with DMPO technique was employed to further verify the reactive oxygen species generated over 3 wt % CQDs/BTO composites photocatalysts [39,40]. In Fig. 10 , the strenuous characteristic peaks of DMPO-superoxide radical (O2
-) were observed for the CQDs/BTO material, which suggested that the O2
- was produced through O2 reduction via one-electron transfer. Moreover, the highly O2
- intensity of CQDs/BTO composites illuminating the introduction of CQDs enable the more photogenerated electrons to reduce O2. This was drew attention to the delocalized conjugated structure of CQDs making it easier to transfer the photogenerated electrons, resulting in efficient electron-hole pair separation. However, for the ·OH, it was not the main reactive species for the CQDs/BTO materials.

In order to further explore the roles of the active species on the photocatalysis process over CQDs/BTO composites, the experiments were then carried out by adding different scavengers N2 for O2
-, isopropyl alcohol (IPA) for ·OH and disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA-2Na) for hole. As shown in Fig. 11 , when the N2 or EDTA-2Na was added, the degradation efficiency was great inhibited, yet when the IPA was added, the photodegradation efficiency was not obvious changed. This result indicated that the O2
- and holes were main active species, but the ·OH was not main active species, during the photocatalysis process. The result of active trapping experiments was consistent with the above analysis.

Based on the results of photodegradation and reactive oxygen species during the degradation process, Fig. 12 illustrated the possible photocatalytic mechanism schematics. When the CQDs/BTO nanosheets were irradiated by sunlight, the BTO can be easily excited with photogenerated electrons on the CB and leaving the holes on the VB. The excited electrons could spatial transfer to the CQDs quickly and improve efficient electron-hole pair separation. After that, the separated electrons on the CQDs could react with O2 to produce more O2
- and the CQDs acted as catalytic sites. The generated O2
- and the holes play the main role in the photodegradation process, causing in excellent photocatalytic activity.

In summary, a green and economical CQDs were synthesized for bamboo and coupled with BTO nanosheets as a novel photocatalysts for degradation. The FT-IR analysis suggested that CQDs coupled with BTO nanosheets. The photocatalytic performance of the CQDs/BTO composite exhibited excellent improvement than pure BTO. The enhanced photocurrent intensity and the reduced PL intensity showed that the CQDs could effectively inhibited the recombination of electrons-holes. Such a green and economical CQDs materials and method to enhance the photocatalytic activity will be spread in other system.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Science Foundation of China (21676115, U1510126, U1662125), Jiangsu Provincial Natural Science Foundation (BK20140527), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2016M590418, 2015M571683, 2017M611732, 2017M611716) and Innovation Programs Foundation of Jiangsu Province (KYLX16_0877).
